---
title: "Differential expression analysis of MOV10 over-expression in HEK293F cells"
author: "Shaun Webb"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    output-file: index.html
---

In this study, we investigate the effects of **MOV10** overexpression in *HEK293F* cells using `DESeq2`.

## Introduction

The data from this analysis is part of a larger study described in [Kenny PJ et al., Cell Rep 2014](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25464849/).

![](https://bioconductor.posit.co/images/logo/svg/Logo.svg)

The RNA-seq analysis was performed using the following steps:

1.  Quality control with [fastqc](https://www.bioinformatics.babraham.ac.uk/projects/fastqc/)
2.  Adapter trimming with [cutadapt](https://cutadapt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)
3.  Pseudo-alignment with [salmon](https://combine-lab.github.io/salmon/) to the human transcriptome (GRCh38 Ensembl release 106).

The DESeq2 dataset contains counts for the following samples:

| Dataset    | Description           | Replicate   |
|------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| Control_1  | Control               | replicate 1 |
| Control_2  | Control               | replicate 2 |
| Control_3  | Control               | replicate 3 |
| MOV10_OE_1 | MOV10 over-expression | replicate 1 |
| MOV10_OE_2 | MOV10 over-expression | replicate 2 |
| MOV10_OE_3 | MOV10 over-expression | replicate 3 |

## Data analysis

### Load libraries and dataset

```{r load_libraries}
#| message: true
#| warning: true

# Load the libraries we will use in this analysis
library(tidyverse)
library(DESeq2)
library(plotly)
library(pheatmap)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(plotly)
library(apeglm)
library(DT)
library(ggrepel)

# Load the DESeq2 dataset
dds <- readRDS("data/MOV10_dds.rds")
# Display the column data from our dataset
colData(dds)
```

### Run DESeq2

```{r run_deseq2}
## run DESeq
dds <- DESeq(dds)
```

### Quality control

The dispersion estimates are used to model the variance in the data. The plot below shows the dispersion estimates for each gene in the dataset.

```{r plot_dispersion}
#| fig-cap: "Dispersion estimates for each gene in the dataset"
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

#Plot dispersions
plotDispEsts(dds, main="Dispersion plot")
```

The heatmap below shows the distances between samples in the dataset. The distance is calculated using the Euclidean distance between the samples based on the log-transformed counts.

```{r heatmap_sample_distance}
#| fig-cap: "Heatmap of sample distances"
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

## Log transformed data
rld <- rlog(dds, blind=F)

sampleDists <- dist(t(assay(rld))) ## t() function transposes a matrix
sampleDistMatrix <- as.matrix(sampleDists)
rownames(sampleDistMatrix) <- as.list(colnames(dds))
colnames(sampleDistMatrix) <- as.list(colnames(dds))
cols <- colorRampPalette( rev(brewer.pal(9, "Blues")) )(255) ## Set a colour pallette in shades of blue
pheatmap(sampleDistMatrix,
         clustering_distance_rows = sampleDists,
         clustering_distance_cols = sampleDists,
         col = cols)
```

PCA analysis is a dimensionality reduction technique that allows us to visualize the variation in the dataset. The plot below shows the first two principal components of the dataset.

```{r pca_plot}
#| fig-cap: "PCA plot of the dataset"
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

## Principle component analysis - get the PCA data
pca <- plotPCA(rld, intgroup = c("Group", "Replicate"), returnData = T)

## Plot with ggplot
pca <- ggplot(pca, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Group, shape = Replicate, text = paste0(Group,"_",Replicate))) + 
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_bw() + 
  theme(legend.key = element_blank()) + 
  xlab(paste("PC1:",round(attr(pca,"percentVar")[1]*100),"%")) + 
  ylab(paste("PC2:",round(attr(pca,"percentVar")[2]*100),"%"))

pca
```

The plot below is an interactive version of the PCA plot above. You can hover over the points to see the sample names.

```{r pca_plotly}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-cap: "Volcano plot MOV10 vs Control"
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 6

ggplotly(pca, tooltip = "text")
```

The heatmap below shows the top 20 most variable genes in the dataset. The genes are clustered based on their expression patterns across the samples.

```{r heatmap_top_genes}
#| fig-cap: "Heatmap of the top 20 most variable genes"
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 6

## Get the top 20 genes after ordering the rld counts by variance
topVarGenes <- head(order(-rowVars(assay(rld),useNames = T)), 20)

## Create a matrix from these genes only
mat <- assay(rld)[topVarGenes, ] 
## Create an annotation dataframe for the columns (samples) to show the Group and Replicate information
anno <- as.data.frame(colData(dds)[,c("Group","Replicate")])
## Plot the heatmap with pheatmap
pheatmap(mat, cluster_rows = T, cluster_cols = T, show_rownames = T, scale = "row", annotation_col = anno)
```

### Results MOV10 over-expression vs Control

The results below show the differential expression analysis between MOV10 over-expression and control samples.

We use the `lfcShrink` function to shrink the log2 fold changes for better visualisation and interpretation and set a significance threshold of 0.05 for the adjusted p-value.

```{r results}
#| warning: false
#| message: false

## Set significance threshold
alpha = 0.05

## Get the results for the comparison of interest
resLFC <- lfcShrink(dds, coef = "Group_MOV10_OE_vs_Control", type = "apeglm")

## Output a summary of results
summary(resLFC, alpha = alpha)
```

The table below shows the results of the differential expression analysis. The results are sorted by the adjusted p-value (padj) in ascending order.

```{r results_table}
#| warning: false
#| message: false

## Read in the gene annotation file to get gene symbols for the Ensembl gene IDs
gene_map <- read_tsv("data/salmon_tx2gene.tsv",col_names = c("TxID","GeneID","GeneSymbol")) |>
  dplyr::select(GeneID, GeneSymbol) |>
  unique()

## Set a threshold for log2 fold change to call significant genes by effect size as well as padj
lthresh = 1

## Build the result table
result_table <- resLFC |>
    as.data.frame() |> 
    rownames_to_column("GeneID") |> 
    left_join(gene_map, by = "GeneID") |> 
    mutate(Significant = case_when( 
      padj <= alpha & abs(log2FoldChange) >= lthresh ~ "Both",
      padj <= alpha & abs(log2FoldChange) < lthresh ~ "padj",
      padj > alpha & abs(log2FoldChange) >= lthresh ~ "l2FC",
      .default = "Not Significant")
      ) |> 
   mutate(Significant = factor(Significant, levels = c("Not Significant", "padj", "l2FC", "Both"))) |>
   arrange(padj) |> 
   as_tibble()

## Output as datatable for interactive viewing
DT::datatable(result_table,
              options = list(pageLength = 10, 
                             scrollX = TRUE), 
              rownames = FALSE)
              
```

### Significant results

This table only shows genes with a p_adj value < `r alpha` and a log2FC threshold of `r lthresh`.

```{r sig_table}
sig_table <- result_table |> filter(Significant == "Both")
  
DT::datatable(sig_table,options = list(pageLength = 10, 
                             scrollX = TRUE), 
              rownames = FALSE)
```

## Visualisation

#### Volcano Plot

The volcano plot below shows the log2 fold change (x-axis) versus the -log10 adjusted p-value (y-axis) for each gene in the dataset. The genes are coloured based on their significance.

```{r volcano_plot}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-cap: "Volcano plot MOV10 vs Control"
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 6

sig_cols <- c("Not Significant" = "grey20", "padj" = "#143960", "l2FC" = "#08A4BD", "Both" = "#C91F3A")

topTen <- result_table |> head(n = 10)

ggplot(result_table, aes(log2FoldChange, -log10(padj), colour = Significant)) + 
  geom_point(size = 1, alpha = 0.5) + 
  geom_text_repel(data = topTen, aes(label = GeneSymbol))+
  theme_bw() + 
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(sig_cols)) +
  ggtitle("MOV10_OE_vs_Control") +
  guides(colour = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size=3, alpha = 1)))
```

### MA plot

The MA plot shows the normalised read counts (M) on the x-axis and the log2 fold change (A) of each gene on the y-axis.

```{r ma_plot}
plotMA(resLFC)
```
